This is trickier than it appears. From a scan of the references, most callers build the command using an f-string:

https://github.com/SFDO-Tooling/CumulusCI/blob/46ae50dcbe404f443fe9da7d70074cb398ab47a1/cumulusci/core/config/sfdx_org_config.py#L169

Without using `shlex.split` or its equivalent we won't have any options (args) to print. The other tricky bit is that the most common way to invoke SFDX, `SFDXOrgTask`, doesn't use `sfdx` at all, it inherits from `SFDXBaseTask`:

https://github.com/SFDO-Tooling/CumulusCI/blob/46ae50dcbe404f443fe9da7d70074cb398ab47a1/cumulusci/tasks/sfdx.py#L23-L40

...which inherits from `Command`, which has its own `sarge.Command`:

https://github.com/SFDO-Tooling/CumulusCI/blob/46ae50dcbe404f443fe9da7d70074cb398ab47a1/cumulusci/tasks/command.py#L108-L122

(🐢³)

The path forward here isn't immediately clear to me, so we should probably discuss this before we meet next week.

My only suggestion is that I think we should keep it together with our other config and environment handling rather than in a separate part of the codebase.

The current ones are in [LocalConfig](https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/3f9a7fe8950dad8f057d6ebda6b3c1a025e2c96e/datacube/config.py#L60-L62), and all fields there can be defined in one conf file and kept together. And the other envionment variables are handled in that class too.

(See [DATACUBE_ENVIRONMENT](https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/91e0f0b78fa7aec2947c3cd6a12e5716edbb4dfa/datacube/config.py#L87), and [DATACUBE_DB_URL](https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/91e0f0b78fa7aec2947c3cd6a12e5716edbb4dfa/datacube/config.py#L195) as examples of existing env vars -- both of which override fields that exist in the original config)

Minor things:
- Prefer `DATACUBE_` prefix instead of `ODC_` for consistency with other env variables. Maybe `DATACUBE_IAM_AUTHENTICATION`, as in theory a future index may not be RDS.
- Ideally make that auth token timeout a config option too while we're here -- it feels inevitable that different environments will want to customise it (?)

Looks pretty good, but I would prefer if we can keep this protocol | URI thing purely an internal implementation detail and minimally scoped to avoid issues like the job submission needing to worry about it.

Based on my understanding, we only need this in order to tell the agent which plugin it should call `delete` on when GC happens, right? If that's the case, what if we just do the following:

- Prepend `plugin_name|` to the URIs when we call `get_uris` to pass it into C++-land.
- In the agent, when `DeleteURI` is called, parse out the plugin_name and URI _before_ calling into a specific plugin for deletion.

This should isolate this to just those two specific places instead of needing to inject this logic all over the place. What do you think?

Looking at code around GitHub, there is some code that subclass `NeuralNet` and override `run_single_epoch`. For example:

https://github.com/shivar2/MotorImagery-Classification/blob/deaf89cb77b02b3033595e21bb54b0607e4b4d1a/Code/Classifier/EEGTLClassifier.py#L28

https://github.com/kqf/coin-detector/blob/29c26bd34489a11a381e995de13c5735cb1e6d4a/detectors/detnet.py#L35-L37

Passing in a different object into `run_single_epoch` would be BC breaking for the above. If one writes a custom `fit_loop` and passes the dataset into `run_single_epoch`, that would break as well.

Looking through the `DataLoader` [source](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/8559d39cf032a9118c470ebe3a5047f89d94fe5c/torch/utils/data/dataloader.py#L75) I do not see any costly operations. I agree it would be nice to initialize the loader once, but there is a significant BC cost.

Hey Brian,

I checked the full history of `taskcluster-proxy` in the monorepo, and `taskcluster-proxy` in its original repository, and this appears never to have been the behaviour of `taskcluster-proxy` in the past.

TLDR; I think this is the wrong place to make the fix.

I think whatever broke, broke somewhere else, and this fix may end up causing us more pain than it solves. For example, it looks like this will set the `Content-Type` header for requests that have no body, such as GET requests, which could introduce adverse affects in middleware or caching proxies etc. I think passing through only the headers that are sent, plus the Authorization header, is the correct approach, and I suspect whatever was calling taskcluster-proxy is the thing that broke, rather than taskcluster-proxy itself.

I realise this might sound like I'm splitting hairs, but it feels like this change will mask problems elsewhere and could lead to unexpected results. For example, calling code that doesn't set the header when it runs through the proxy, wouldn't work if it was pointed at the taskcluster endpoint directly. The clients should all set the `Content-Type` header when it is required, so if it is getting missed somewhere, we have a different problem that this change would mask.

I think the next steps are to do a deep dive to understand what is not passing the Content-Type header in a request to the proxy (whether it is something in a task, something in docker-worker, or something in a taskcluster client) and hopefully we can isolate the component that introduces the issue, and find the commit that broke it, and hopefully find the smoking gun! ☁️ 🔫

We should fix this issue by making the raylet side idempotent (not by modifying gcs placement group scheduler). Check node manager.cc's line 2086. 

It is specified in the design doc of atmoic creation. I haven't implemented idempotency of the raylet's prepare / remove / and commit endpoints (that's why this failure happened). 
https://docs.google.com/document/d/1tPMs2W-557hPmln5xnycK85vKTxJujZIsjq9Rsw9OzY/edit#

It is because when the lease is cancelled, we will destroy bundles "after" we commit them (look at line 480 of gcs_placement_group_scheduler.cc). This is to make the logic simple and straightforward than having many different check inbetween async IO operations (so, basically to avoid situation we went through when we developed actor manager code).

Looks ok. As far as I see there are a couple of use cases we would like to cover:

- When we release the package to PyPI we call `sentinelhub.config --clear` and then upload the package. So if `config.json` wouldn't exist then `sentinelhub.config --clear` would trigger its creation. Therefore `config.json` will always be released together with the package and anyone who istalls it with pip will have it.
- If a developer clones a git repo and install the package e.g. with `pip install -e .` then `config.json` won't be created automatically. But often during development it's convenient to have it so that you can just copy-paste credentials into it.

Because of the 2nd case I suggest adding the following function into `setup.py`

```Python
def try_create_config_file():
    """ After the package is installed it will try to trigger saving a config.json file
    """
    try:
        from sentinelhub.config import SHConfig
        SHConfig()
    except BaseException:
        pass
```
and calling it at the end after the `setup` is called. I checked that this then `config.json` automatically right after a package is installed.

@Frassle @RobbieMcKinstry thank you for your (very prompt!) feedback!

Based on @Frassle's comment I modified this to no longer set defaults during import etc. Now we'll _only_ set Created+Updated during a create, and Updated during an update. With this behavior it's possible and very likely for a resource to have Updated set with a nil Created. This is reasonable if we expect these to represent an exact timestamp and not a best guess.

I took a stab at a lifecycle test that goes through create → refresh → update steps. The timestamps _appear_ to propagate correctly but I feel uncertain. Is there some pathological case to consider that could accidentally set these to nil? Do reads matter? Failed operations? 🤔 

Folks using older CLI releases will of course reset these to nil -- that's unavoidable, and also a problem that lessens over time as people upgrade. We _might_ be able to detect this situation on the SaaS and generate some server-side timestamps, but I haven't looked into it yet.

I feel like the tests are still getting too deep into the details and verifying a lot of implementation details via mocking. For example, we should not need to rely on implementing specific methods of the controller like `listen_for_change` (as a smell test, this means if the long poll code changes the method name it uses, we'd need to update the proxy tests).

Fundamentally the proxies do two things: (1) receive requests over HTTP/gRPC and (2) proxy those requests over the `ServeHandle` interface. For the purpose of these unit tests, there are already clear interfaces defined for (1) (gRPC and ASGI). For (2), I think we need to define a slightly higher-level interface than what we currently have so that it can be faked for testing to test the proxy implementation without delving into the details too much.

This might look something like:

```
class ProxyHandle:
    async def send_request_to_replica_unary(self):
        # Returns OK/error/timeout.

    async def send_request_to_replica_streaming(self):
        # Is a generator yielding ASGI / gRPC messages.
```

The production implementation of this will use a `ServeHandle`, but for tests you can see that we could easily manipulate a fake implementation to test the behavior of the proxy class under various conditions.

Note that this might be slightly too high-level of an interface to test some logic such as timeouts. That could either go within that class (and have its own unit tests), or the interface can be made lower level to accommodate this.

I want to push on this because I think it's going to make our lives much easier down the line, but given the time pressure for branch cut we can discuss postponing it until after branch cut. Let's chat about it in the morning.

6. Rework `apply_func_to_indices_both_axis` into `map_select_indices_both_axes`

Unfortunately the location of the checks is not correct, and they should be pushed up to the `cpu` / `cuda` functions.

This is because the dispatcher (which currently is only setup for NMS and RoIAlign) don't perform argchecking in the main dispatcher code, and thus those checks are probably going to be skipped.
See the PR porting NMS to use the dispatcher in https://github.com/pytorch/vision/pull/2384/commits/4e8deeb12dced29640efda22c7a12562a2edd375 to see that we had subtle errors and had to duplicate the checks in the cpu / cuda folders.

Taking a comment from @ezyang who pointed out the issue

> but right now, you have to make a helper function and make sure the cpu and cuda functions call that helper function manually
>
> do NOT put size checks in the dispatching function, you are not guaranteed to call that function, and also if you put the checks there you'll do them repeatedly uselessly

*[container_service_extension/consumer/amqp_consumer.py, line 245 at r2](https://reviewable.io/reviews/vmware/container-service-extension/785#-MJXqqR66eE7fpB1Ofmq:-MJXqqR66eE7fpB1Ofmr:b-y7aqw) ([raw file](https://github.com/vmware/container-service-extension/blob/fc4f2ebe00529b966cee332620632a6a89b5b010/container_service_extension/consumer/amqp_consumer.py#L245)):*
> ```Python
>         self.stop_consuming()
>         if self._connection:
>             self._connection.ioloop.stop()
> ```

I think that this logic previously followed the following example: https://pika.readthedocs.io/en/0.13.1/examples/asynchronous_consumer_example.html 

This example called ioloop.start() when the consumer was closing because the consumer was running in the main thread; the thread's ioloop.start() was interrupted during a KeyboardInterrupt, so ioloop.start() during close to restart the connection and it would close on its own. 
In our use case, the consumer is a separately spawned thread, so we do not call ioloop.start() during close sine this ioloop was not interrupted, but we close the connection.

Functionally, this is sound. Depending on the response to my comment, this PR might still need changes though.

> Open questions:
> 1. Should `BSPCompileRequest` take multiple `FieldSet` values? This would allow the language backend to merge compilation via `CoarsenedTargets` since the ;language backend would see all of the relevant field sets together. I'm leaning toward that as the better approach than the current implementation in this PR.

In practice, I'm not sure that it will matter...? `CoarsenedTarget` is deterministic regardless of entrypoint, so if you have two files within one cycle, they will always "become" the same `CT`, and trigger the same compilation. As the API is now, that would mean two roots depending on the same inner compilation node.

Depending on how you're rendering outputs, merging might prevent redundant rendering of compilation outputs...? Our logging of `CompileResult` will already dedupe, but if you need to send specific per-request output, merging might make more sense.

> 2. Is there a way to get absolute paths into the Coursier cache? (So BSP can provide the scalac classpath to IntelliJ.) If not, then that resolution will need to be written out just like classfiles are currently by the PR.

There is. But I'm not sure that it's worth a special case. Note too that if BSP supports jars (rather than loose classfiles) as artifacts for that case, it would be surprising if it didn't support them for per-target compilation.

> 3. Big question is what should be in `classpath` returned by the `buildTarget/scalacOptions` BSP method. I'm going to ask on the intellij-scala Discord about it. At the very least, I am going to split the classfiles directories of different resolves. (And we will probably need to write out the jars of a resolve to a stable location just like for scalac classpath if there is no way to get paths into the Coursier cache that can be shared with IntelliJ, but that is not for this PR.)

See my question re: semantics below. I expect that the entire loaded "project" should be one resolve.

Did a usage first pass, I have a few high-level pubsub design questions before I dig deeper.

It looks like we're using a channel abstraction in order to be able to piggyback off of shared publisher <--> subscriber long-polling connections, where the publisher and subscriber interfaces expose the channel concept to the application code via the channel type and deal only with a generic `PubMessage` message type that oneofs across the messages for the different channels. This then entails pass-through methods to the subinterfaces for each channel, maintaining channel --> data structure maps internally, and leaking what is essentially a transport detail (the long-polling connection) to the application level by requiring publishers/subscribers to specify the channel type. I'm wondering if there's a better way to structure this such that the application code doesn't have to worry about channels and more of this logic is encoded in the type system so we have compile-time guarantees that things are 👌 .

What if we adopted a pattern similar to gRPC's shared transport channel, generalized from managing a 1-to-1 connection to managing 1-to-n connections, where we have a transport broker concept that encapsulates the publisher <--> subscriber long-polling connections. The subscriber interface for each pubsub instance takes a shared pointer to the broker on construction, and can therefore share the broker across different pubsub instances (e.g. separate subscriber objects for `WAIT_FOR_OBJECT_EVICTION` and `WAIT_FOR_REF_REMOVED`), where new publisher <--> subscriber connections can be registered by any of the pubsub instances at subscription time.

```
subscribers <--> broker <--transport--> broker <--> publishers
```

I think there could be a few advantages here:

1. We could then expose a subscriber interface per pubsub instance, templated on the concrete message type (no need for a proto oneof message to be exposed to the application code, that could be isolated to the broker), which will give better compile-time checking of the application-level pubsub messages and will obviate the need for those passthrough methods in a top-level subscriber interface that requires a channel type to be specified.
2. I think that you'd still need a proto-level channel type enum, but I think that you could get away with specializing the subscriber with _the enum value itself_. E.g. `Subscriber<typename MessageID, typename Message, rpc::ChannelType channel_type>` could be specialized as `Subscriber<ObjectID, rpc::WaitForObjectEvictionMessage, rpc::WAIT_FOR_OBJECT_EVICTION>`, where the `channel_type` is included when interacting with the broker (e.g. subscribe, unsubscribe). Dispatch of messages received in the long-poll response by the broker will still have to use a channel --> subscriber table, but that can be populated dynamically when the subscriber is constructed: `broker_.RegisterSubscriber(channel_type, this)`.
3. The transport-level optimization of sharing long-polling connections is pushed down as an implementation detail of the transport broker concept, and is a natural place to evolve other transport-level details, like switching to a bidi streaming connection or switching to a centralized message broker.
4. Other transport-level things, like the core worker client pool and the subscriber address/port, can be moved to the broker. Publisher addresses will probably continue to be required by subscriber methods, unless we want the broker to always map worker or node IDs to concrete addresses (probably less efficient in some cases?).
5. The current process of registering a new channel will be replaced by adding a new channel type to the proto enum, adding the channel's proto to the broker's pubsub message oneof, templating a subscriber with a new message type and the channel type, and reusing the existing broker. I don't think there should be much more required than that.

I think that you would have an analogous broker abstraction on the publisher side as well (encapsulating the cached long-polling connection, controlling the message batching), although I haven't thought it through as much since I think it should be simpler than the subscriber-side. My guess is that the publisher would be similarly templated, would similarly register itself with the publish-side broker on construction, and each publisher instance (one per channel type) would have its own subscription index. I think that the publisher's `Subscriber` would be internal to the publish-side broker.

The cons with this broker approach, as I see them:

1. Broker concept is exposed to the application level, although what's needed to configure that concept (worker client pool, subscriber address/port, etc.) is already exposed to the application level, so I'm not sure if this is any worse?
2. A shared transport-level broker will obviously have to be thread-safe, although that shouldn't be very difficult.
3. Hidden dragons that I haven't thought of.

Lmk what you think about this approach and whether I'm missing anything! This is also something that could wait until a future PR, e.g. when I port the OBOD object location subscriptions or the GCS node/actor tables to this pubsub I'm sure that I'll have to refactor some stuff anyway.

I'm not sure changing the dictionary order is a good way to handle this. While it will work and it's fine in CPython, pure python itself doesn't guarantee the order of a dictionary, dicts are supposed to be an inherently unordered data type, and in other implementations of python, such as Pypy, iterating through the dict may result in a different order than the one in which the dict was defined.

Even in CPython it's not guaranteed that this current ordered behavior of dicts will be preserved in future versions. Instead, it may be wise to use `collections.OrderedDict`, or an approach with 2 separate dictionaries, checking the first one before the other one. In fact, in previous CPython versions, this was not the case and dict order was essentially random.

Just a few comments before merging.

1. The `docs/auto_examples` folder is auto generated by a sphinx plugin I've used called sphinx-gallery.  So the original files related to this folder that you need to change are under the folder `tests/examples/`.
2. The changes on `statemachine/callbacks.py` and `statemachine/diagram.py` are not following the automated enforced style guide.  As I'm still working on porting the running automated tests as GH actions on the PRs, I need you to run them locally.

See https://python-statemachine.readthedocs.io/en/develop/contributing.html#get-started to get the reference on how to setup locally.

These are the required steps:

1. Run [poetry](https://python-poetry.org/docs/#installation) ``install`` to install all the dependencies and create a virtual environment:

        poetry install

2. Install the pre-commit validations:

        pre-commit install

2. You can run the pre-commit validations by hand, and the files will be automatically updated by black/ruff:

        pre-commit run --all-files

3. You may need to get a fresh docs build folder, to renew the `auto_examples`, 
    you can safely remove these directories to get a clean state by running:

        rm -rf docs/_build/ docs/auto_examples

4. Run the build again:

        poetry run sphinx-autobuild docs docs/_build/html --re-ignore "auto_examples/.*"

Let me know if you have any trouble with this.

Thanks again for your effort on this!

Best regards!

Thanks for submitting a PR but as it stands currently, this hurts UX in the general case. This captures the output of the Black invocation and never reemits it. That's already not great, but it gets worse. If Black errors out for some reason, the developer reading the logs will have no idea what's wrong since the logs will be almost entirely empty. There are two ways of fixing this:

- Reemit the captured output (always or on a non-zero return code; I'd prefer the former)  
- Tee or somehow duplicate the output stream so it can be captured _and_ printed to the console as usual
 
The second could preserve the colour output and allow for live feedback (unlike the first option), however, I have never done this before so no clue whether this is possible. ASNI codes make it sound really annoying.

I know this Action's output could be improved in general, but that's dependant on GitHub fixing their own infrastructure (which will never happen IME), but if it's possible let's not make it worse.

Also, the general convention for GitHub Actions are to use dashes instead of underscores.  [Here's an example.](https://github.com/marketplace/actions/close-stale-issues).

Finally, I'm not keen on using using numbers as boolean, we could use `true` / `false` instead. I can go either way on this point though.

I know this is a lot of words, but I do really mean it that I appreciate the effort you've put into this. I'm just one of those "let's right it the first time" folks (un)fortunately :)

my biggest concern currently is the change to the serializer API, heres some high-level feedback:

* conceptually it seems odd to have to pass aspects of the _request_ (the request headers) to serialize a _response_, maybe we could make this more high-level (ideas below).
* moreover, we now have multiple control paths in the same serializer dealing with different output formats. again, conceptually it seems cleaner to have specific serializer instances for specific output formats, rather than hiding the output format inside a single serializer.

I'm stating this as a bit of a matter of record about how/why we're changing this. Please correct any of this if I'm misstating our discussion.

---

We discussed the fact that `--administrator/--no-administrators` diverges from the existing two patterns for managing a list field (including clearing it).
Those are exemplified by options to `globus collection update`. Specifically, (1) `--sharing-users-allow ""` and (2) `--keywords ""`. (1) is that we accept a `multiple=True` string field and convert `""` to mean "clear the field". (2) is that we accept a `CommaDelimitedList` and convert `""` to mean "clear".

After a lengthy discussion, we agreed on the merits of (2) as the overall approach, changing the option string to `--administrators`. We also agreed that `globus flows create` should be updated in future work to match.

The important item here in terms of direction and interface for the CLI is that we are modeling list fields as `CommaDelimitedList`. I would go so far as to state that this is a norm we are now establishing and which we can follow and converge towards (where possible) in the future.

CSV has the advantage that the empty list is naturally encoded as `""`.

Thanks for the PR.

Just some minor bikeshedding/comments for the first pass:

1. `AppCommandContext` is *not* an enum, it's actually a flag type. Discord has recently been doing bitfields using an array because they falsely believe it leads to better developer experience since they are targeting developers that do not know how to bitshift. I suggest migrating this over to use the new `ArrayFlags` type and work with it. I have tried to get them to change this to a regular integer but they seem to want to not want to change it to the more space-efficient and sane format.
2. `allowed_contexts` and any external reference to `context` in this well *context* should probably be called `restrict` instead. The type should still be called `AppCommandContext` but the attribute (and maybe eventual decorator) should be called `restrict`.
3. `private_only` should probably be renamed to `private_channel_only` for consistency with the library (e.g. `abc.PrivateChannel`).
4. The flag names for `AppCommandContext` should not use the Discord naming scheme since they are bad, instead it should probably be `guild`, `dm_channel`, and `private_channel`.

i think there are some conceptual things to clarify before we continue working on this.

i can make out two key ideas in this PRs:
* logging requests as they arrive, rather than as part of the response logger
* additional output for helping developers understand requests

in principle i find both of those ideas good.


however there are multiple problems with the PR in its current state.

* the logging becomes very verbose. we're adding `Can reproduce in CLI with: awslocal ...` for *every* request, which seems a bit excessive to me tbh. i very rarely care about this, and can see that this will be particularly annoying in CI logs.
* we're logging requests twice now. once as it comes in, and then as it goes through the response logger. i don't think that's helpful
* we're calling `restore_payload` for *every* request, which is a relatively expensive operation and should only be called when really necessary
* this doesn't seem to work for services that use the query protocol. for example:
`awslocal sqs list-queues` translates to 
`AWS request to sqs.ListQueues. Can reproduce in CLI with: awslocal sqs list-queues --region us-east-1 --cli-input-json 'Action=ListQueues&Version=2012-11-05'`, and that CLI command will obviously not work since it's not json.

key idea 1: if we want to split request/repsonse logging in a generic way), i think we need to spend a bit more time thinking about what the log output should look like. logging a request twice is too verbose IMO. if we separate request and response logging, then we need to a) completely rework the log formatters in `format.py`, and b) introduce a way to associate requests to responses in the logs (with request IDs), and add those to the logs. otherwise it won't be possible to know which request belongs to which response.  @dfangl and i spent quite a bit of time thinking about logging for the v1 release, and found that to not be a good way forward. as i said, i'm not against it per-se, if you can come up with a good solution. however this seems to be a bit out of scope of this PR, since it will be quite a significant rework.

key idea 2: i would be OK merging this if we keep this logger behind an opt-in feature flag, something like `LOG_AWS_REQUEST_CLI_REPRODUCERS=1` (or something specific along those lines), and the problems with `LS_LOG=trace` and the other AWS protocols are fixed.

in summary, i would try to make the PR more specific to key idea 2. by first fixing all the issues, and then move away from the idea to introduce a second generic request logging approach.

Added some comments where I saw that the code style or general usage diverges too far from the current standard or where we already have specific patterns in place.

One issue I've noticed is that the resource cleanup needs to be adjusted to be more robust. A typical example for a resource leak here would be:
```
# 1. create secret
# 2. do something with secret
# 3. cleanup (delete secret)
```

If anything in step 2 fails, the cleanup will be skipped and resources start leaking. This affects LocalStack as well but more importantly your AWS account might end up cluttered with these.

There are a few patterns for this in place right now:

1. Create a fixture
2. Using the `cleanups` fixture and appending cleanup callables (in the simplest case just a lambda performing the same cleanup you'd otherwise do)
3. try/finally blocks (these additionally need to account for the fact that when cleaning up multiple resources, subsequent resource cleanup has to stay unaffected)

Option 1/2 are in general preferred over 3
